text: The surgeon performs a left lower lobectomy, removing the left lower lobe of the lung.
The surgeon dissects pleural adhesions at the site of the mini thoracotomy using a blunt instrument or hook, carefully separating the adhesions from surrounding tissue.
The video shows the lung surface after adhesiolysis, revealing areas where pleural adhesions have been dissected, with visible changes in the lung's appearance.
The surgeon dissects the pulmonary ligament, harvests the station IX lymph node, and identifies the inferior border of the inferior pulmonary vein.
The surgeon sections the anterior and posterior mediastinal pleura, revealing the superior border of the inferior pulmonary vein in preparation for its dissection.
The surgeon prepares for a laborious dissection in the fissure, ensuring to complete the arterial dissection before stapling the vein to avoid vascular tension.
The surgeon releases the fissure and identifies the pulmonary artery, preparing for further dissection in the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon uses a hook and scissors to complete the dissection of the fissure, ensuring clear separation of the tissue planes.
The surgeon exposes the pulmonary artery within the fissure, clearly visible amidst the surrounding tissue.
The interlobar lymph node is visible, positioned between the lower lobe bronchus and the branches of the pulmonary artery.

The surgeon encounters tight adhesions of lymphadenopathy to the bronchial and vascular structures, necessitating the use of both blunt dissection and hook electrocautery to carefully separate these tissues.
The surgeon ensures the arterial branches are free of lymph nodes and completes the vascular dissection by applying a 35mm wide end of a stapler cartridge to each vessel.
Following arterial stapling, the lymph node is visible along with surrounding anatomical structures, including the pulmonary artery and bronchial branches.
The surgeon pauses at the left lower bronchus, where the lymph node is situated adjacent to the branches of the pulmonary artery.
The surgeon uses a curved tip stapler reload to facilitate the passage of the stapler under the inferior pulmonary vein, preparing for vessel stapling.
The surgeon visually inspects the stump of the inferior pulmonary vein and the left lower lobe bronchus, which are exposed in the surgical field.
The surgeon releases the bronchus by completing the interlobar lymphadenectomy, followed by the final dissection of the bronchus using a 45mm green endostapler reload.
The surgeon checks the inflation of the upper lobe, indicated by a blue arrow, before firing the stapler.
The surgeon completes the mediastinal and hilar lymphadenectomy, followed by the insertion of a 24 Fr Sharier chest tube into the pleural cavity through the working incision for drainage.
